ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai-style chicken; broader 'chicken dish' term used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai-style chicken dish; A0CKN (cooked chicken meat) used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Thai-style chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Thai Style Chicken is approximately 200g, equivalent to one restaurant-sized portion of chicken pieces with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Style Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai Style Chicken (cr_id G00010275), confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed chicken dish with sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai Style Chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed chicken dish with moderate sauce and vegetable components from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 7.17g/100g; GL per 200g serving = (45 × 14.34) / 100 = 6.45, rounded to 6.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 753mg/100g — well above the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Thai Style Chicken is not suitable for individuals with hypertension due to very high sodium content (753mg/100g) driven by fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce; patients should request reduced-sauce preparations or limit portion size to no more than 100g per meal and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.17g/100g) and low sugar (1.26g/100g) with moderate protein supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai Style Chicken is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its low carbohydrate (7.17g/100g) and low sugar (1.26g/100g) content; however, it should not be paired with white rice or other high-GI staples, and patients should confirm sugar content with the vendor as some Thai preparations add significant amounts of sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (753mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat (approximately 4.2g/100g from coconut milk) present dual cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thai Style Chicken poses moderate cardiovascular risk due to high sodium content (753mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat from coconut milk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request reduced-sauce and reduced-coconut-milk preparations, and account for this dish within their daily sodium and saturated fat budgets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herbs (Thai basil, lemongrass, galangal) are beneficial, but high sodium (753mg/100g) worsens tissue swelling and nightshade ingredients (chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thai Style Chicken contains polyphenol-rich anti-inflammatory herbs that may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients, but the high sodium content (753mg/100g) can worsen joint swelling and the presence of nightshade ingredients (chilli) warrants individual monitoring; reduced-sauce preparations are strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Style Chicken is a Southeast Asian chicken dish prepared in the Thai culinary tradition, featuring crispy or stir-fried chicken pieces coated or served with a savoury chili-based sauce. The dish typically incorporates aromatic Thai herbs and condiments including lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai basil, garlic, fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and coconut milk, producing a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously spicy, savoury, and mildly sweet. The texture contrasts crispy chicken exterior with a rich, glossy sauce. Per 100g, the dish provides 172 kcal, 12.13g protein, 10.65g fat, and 7.17g carbohydrate, with notably high sodium at 753mg — a key clinical consideration. Saturated fat is approximately 4.2g/100g from coconut milk. The dish is low in sugar (1.26g/100g) and contains moderate dietary fibre (1.2g/100g). It is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments, but is not recommended for hypertension patients due to very high sodium content. The dish is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner in Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants, often accompanied by steamed jasmine rice. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free but contains soy, fish, and shellfish allergens from the sauce components.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai style chicken?
Can diabetics eat Thai style chicken?
Is Thai style chicken high in sodium?
Thai style chicken suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for all macros and key micronutrients. The food record is canonical and reviewed. Saturated fat estimated from known coconut milk contribution consistent with HPB data patterns. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this Thai-style chicken dish preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Saturated fat value of 4.2g/100g is carried forward from the input health_suitability reasoning and is consistent with coconut milk contribution in Thai chicken dishes; this should be confirmed against the full HPB nutrient panel if available. Cultural metadata in the input incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'Filipino' — corrected to 'Thai' based on the dish name, description, and ingredient profile. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on a comparable mixed chicken dish with moderate sauce and vegetable components; no direct GI measurement exists for Thai Style Chicken. GL per 200g serving calculated as (45 × 14.34) / 100 = 6.45, rounded to 6.4.
